1	"""
2	Pure-Python consent domain service.
3	No Django ORM imports -- works with plain values / dicts.
4	"""
5	from datetime import date, datetime
6	
7	from domain.exceptions import ConflictError, ValidationError
8	
9	
10	def compute_consent_status(
11	    *,
12	    is_revoked: bool,
13	    revoked_at: datetime | None = None,
14	    expiration_date: date | None = None,
15	    effective_date: date | None = None,
16	) -> str:
17	    """
18	    Determine the current status of a consent record.
19	
20	    Returns one of: "active", "expired", "revoked".
21	    """
22	    if is_revoked:
23	        return "revoked"
24	
25	    today = date.today()
26	
27	    if expiration_date and expiration_date < today:
28	        return "expired"
29	
30	    if effective_date and effective_date > today:
31	        # Not yet effective -- still considered "active" (pending start)
32	        return "active"
33	
34	    return "active"
35	
36	
37	def validate_consent_dates(
38	    effective_date: date,
39	    expiration_date: date | None,
40	) -> None:
41	    """
42	    Validate that the expiration date (if provided) is after the
43	    effective date.
44	
45	    Raises domain.exceptions.ValidationError on failure.
46	    """
47	    if expiration_date is not None and expiration_date <= effective_date:
48	        raise ValidationError(
49	            "Expiration date must be after the effective date."
50	        )
51	
52	
53	def validate_revocation(
54	    *,
55	    is_revoked: bool,
56	    physical_copy_on_file: bool,
57	    acknowledged_warning: bool,
58	) -> None:
59	    """
60	    Validate that a consent can be revoked.
61	
62	    Raises ConflictError if already revoked.
63	    Raises ValidationError if a physical-copy warning acknowledgement is
64	    required but was not provided.
65	    """
66	    if is_revoked:
67	        raise ConflictError("This consent has already been revoked.")
68	
69	    if physical_copy_on_file and not acknowledged_warning:
70	        raise ValidationError(
71	            "A physical copy of this consent is on file. "
72	            "You must acknowledge the physical_copy_warning before revoking."
73	        )
74	
75	
76	def validate_consent_for_media(
77	    *,
78	    is_revoked: bool,
79	    expiration_date: date | None = None,
80	    effective_date: date | None = None,
81	    scope_types: list[str] | None = None,
82	    consent_patient_id: str | None = None,
83	    target_patient_id: str | None = None,
84	) -> None:
85	    """
86	    Validate that a consent record authorises media use.
87	
88	    Checks:
89	    1. Consent is not revoked.
90	    2. Consent is not expired (expiration_date >= today).
91	    3. Consent effective_date has been reached (<= today).
92	    4. Consent includes a ``media_use`` scope (if scopes provided).
93	    5. If both patient IDs are given, they must match.
94	
95	    Raises ``ValidationError`` on any check failure.
96	    """
97	    if is_revoked:
98	        raise ValidationError(
99	            "Cannot use media under a revoked consent."
100	        )
101	
102	    today = date.today()
103	
104	    if expiration_date is not None and expiration_date < today:
105	        raise ValidationError(
106	            "Cannot use media under an expired consent."
107	        )
108	
109	    if effective_date is not None and effective_date > today:
110	        raise ValidationError(
111	            "Consent is not yet effective."
112	        )
113	
114	    if scope_types is not None and "media_use" not in scope_types:
115	        raise ValidationError(
116	            "Consent does not include a 'media_use' scope."
117	        )
118	
119	    if (
120	        consent_patient_id is not None
121	        and target_patient_id is not None
122	        and str(consent_patient_id) != str(target_patient_id)
123	    ):
124	        raise ValidationError(
125	            "Consent does not belong to the target patient."
126	        )

20	def _create_test_image(color="red", size=(10, 10), fmt="PNG"):
21	    """Create a minimal in-memory PNG image."""
22	    img = Image.new("RGB", size, color=color)
23	    buf = io.BytesIO()
24	    img.save(buf, format=fmt)
25	    buf.seek(0)
26	    buf.name = "test.png"
27	    return buf

44	# Patterns that match common PII / secret value formats in free text.
45	_REDACTION_PATTERNS: list[tuple[re.Pattern, str]] = [
46	    # SSN: 123-45-6789 or 123456789
47	    (re.compile(r"\b\d{3}-\d{2}-\d{4}\b"), "[REDACTED_SSN]"),
48	    (re.compile(r"\b\d{9}\b(?=\s|$|[^0-9])"), "[REDACTED_SSN]"),
49	    # MRN-style: MRN followed by digits (e.g. MRN12345, MRN-12345)
50	    (re.compile(r"\bMRN[:\-]?\s*\d{4,}\b", re.IGNORECASE), "[REDACTED_MRN]"),
51	    # Email addresses
52	    (re.compile(r"\b[A-Za-z0-9._%+\-]+@[A-Za-z0-9.\-]+\.[A-Za-z]{2,}\b"), "[REDACTED_EMAIL]"),
53	    # Bearer / token values: "Bearer <token>" or "token=<value>"
54	    (re.compile(r"Bearer\s+[A-Za-z0-9\-._~+/]+=*", re.IGNORECASE), "Bearer [REDACTED_TOKEN]"),
55	    (re.compile(r"(token|secret|key|password|passwd|pwd)\s*[=:]\s*\S+", re.IGNORECASE), r"\1=[REDACTED]"),
56	    # Long hex strings (32+ chars) that look like keys/hashes
57	    (re.compile(r"\b[0-9a-fA-F]{32,}\b"), "[REDACTED_HEX]"),
58	    # Date of birth patterns: DOB 1990-01-01 or dob: 01/01/1990
59	    (re.compile(r"\b(?:dob|date.of.birth)\s*[=:]\s*\S+", re.IGNORECASE), "[REDACTED_DOB]"),
60	]
61	
62	
63	def _redact_sensitive_values(text: str) -> str:
64	    """Apply pattern-based redaction to free-text fields."""
65	    for pattern, replacement in _REDACTION_PATTERNS:
66	        text = pattern.sub(replacement, text)
67	    return text

Now I have everything I need. Three fixes to make:

### Gap 1: Empty-scope consents bypass scope check + scope value not validated

The problem: `scope_types if scope_types else None` passes `None` when the consent has zero scopes, skipping the check entirely. Also, only `scope_type == "media_use"` is checked, never the `scope_value`.

Fix: Always pass the scope list (never `None`), so an empty-scope consent is rejected. Also accept an optional `scope_values` for value-level checks.

    """
    Validate that a consent record authorises media use.

    Checks:
    1. Consent is not revoked.
    2. Consent is not expired (expiration_date >= today).
    3. Consent effective_date has been reached (<= today).
    4. Consent includes a ``media_use`` scope_type.
    5. If *scope_values* are given, at least one ``media_use`` scope
       value must match (e.g. ``"photograph"``, ``"all"``).
    6. If both patient IDs are given, they must match.

    Raises ``ValidationError`` on any check failure.
    """
    if is_revoked:

    if expiration_date is not None and expiration_date < today:

    if effective_date is not None and effective_date > today:

    if "media_use" not in scope_types:
        raise ValidationError(
            "Consent does not include a 'media_use' scope."
        )

    if scope_values is not None:
        # At least one media_use scope value must match the required values

Wait — the scope_values check I wrote is a tautology (`any(v in scope_values for v in scope_values)` is always true). Let me rethink. The domain function should receive the actual media_use scope values from the consent and optionally a required usage string. But the callers don't currently know what specific media use to check. The real fix is simpler: just remove the `None` guard so empty-scope consents are rejected, and pass the scope values so they're available for future use. Let me simplify:

{"replace_all": false, "file_path": "E:\Hale\Coding\Eaglepoint\Task-89\repo\backend\apps\audit\views_client_logs.py", "old_string": "# Patterns that match common PII / secret value formats in free text.
_REDACTION_PATTERNS: list[tuple[re.Pattern, str]] = [
    # SSN: 123-45-6789 or 123456789
    (re.compile(r"\b\d{3}-\d{2}-\d{4}\b"), "[REDACTED_SSN]"),
    (re.compile(r"\b\d{9}\b(?=\s|$|[^0-9])"), "[REDACTED_SSN]"),
    # MRN-style: MRN followed by digits (e.g. MRN12345, MRN-12345)
    (re.compile(r"\bMRN[:\-]?\s*\d{4,}\b", re.IGNORECASE), "[REDACTED_MRN]"),
    # Email addresses
    (re.compile(r"\b[A-Za-z0-9._%+\-]+@[A-Za-z0-9.\-]+\.[A-Za-z]{2,}\b"), "[REDACTED_EMAIL]"),
    # Bearer / token values: "Bearer <token>" or "token=<value>"
    (re.compile(r"Bearer\s+[A-Za-z0-9\-._~+/]+=*", re.IGNORECASE), "Bearer [REDACTED_TOKEN]"),
    (re.compile(r"(token|secret|key|password|passwd|pwd)\s*[=:]\s*\S+", re.IGNORECASE), r"\1=[REDACTED]"),
    # Long hex strings (32+ chars) that look like keys/hashes
    (re.compile(r"\b[0-9a-fA-F]{32,}\b"), "[REDACTED_HEX]"),
    # Date of birth patterns: DOB 1990-01-01 or dob: 01/01/1990
    (re.compile(r"\b(?:dob|date.of.birth)\s*[=:]\s*\S+", re.IGNORECASE), "[REDACTED_DOB]"),
]


def _redact_sensitive_values(text: str) -> str:
    """Apply pattern-based redaction to free-text fields."""
    for pattern, replacement in _REDACTION_PATTERNS:
        text = pattern.sub(replacement, text)
    return text", "new_string": "# Patterns that match common PII / secret value formats in free text.
_REDACTION_PATTERNS: list[tuple[re.Pattern, str]] = [
    # SSN: 123-45-6789 or 123456789
    (re.compile(r"\b\d{3}-\d{2}-\d{4}\b"), "[REDACTED_SSN]"),
    (re.compile(r"\b\d{9}\b(?=\s|$|[^0-9])"), "[REDACTED_SSN]"),
    # MRN-style: MRN followed by digits (e.g. MRN12345, MRN-12345)
    (re.compile(r"\bMRN[:\-]?\s*\d{4,}\b", re.IGNORECASE), "[REDACTED_MRN]"),
    # Email addresses
    (re.compile(r"\b[A-Za-z0-9._%+\-]+@[A-Za-z0-9.\-]+\.[A-Za-z]{2,}\b"), "[REDACTED_EMAIL]"),
    # US phone numbers: (555) 123-4567, 555-123-4567, +1-555-123-4567
    (re.compile(r"(?:\+?1[-.\s]?)?\(?\d{3}\)?[-.\s]?\d{3}[-.\s]?\d{4}\b"), "[REDACTED_PHONE]"),
    # Credit card patterns: 16-digit groups with optional separators
    (re.compile(r"\b\d{4}[-\s]?\d{4}[-\s]?\d{4}[-\s]?\d{4}\b"), "[REDACTED_CC]"),
    # JWT tokens: three base64url segments separated by dots
    (re.compile(r"\beyJ[A-Za-z0-9_-]{10,}\.[A-Za-z0-9_-]{10,}\.[A-Za-z0-9_-]{10,}"), "[REDACTED_JWT]"),
    # Bearer / token values: "Bearer <token>" or "token=<value>"
    (re.compile(r"Bearer\s+[A-Za-z0-9\-._~+/]+=*", re.IGNORECASE), "Bearer [REDACTED_TOKEN]"),
    (re.compile(r"(token|secret|key|password|passwd|pwd|authorization|cookie|session_id|api_key|access_key|secret_key)\s*[=:]\s*\S+", re.IGNORECASE), r"\1=[REDACTED]"),
    # Long hex strings (32+ chars) that look like keys/hashes
    (re.compile(r"\b[0-9a-fA-F]{32,}\b"), "[REDACTED_HEX]"),
    # Long base64 strings (40+ chars) that look like encoded credentials
    (re.compile(r"\b[A-Za-z0-9+/]{40,}={0,2}\b"), "[REDACTED_BASE64]"),
    # Date of birth patterns: DOB 1990-01-01 or dob: 01/01/1990
    (re.compile(r"\b(?:dob|date.of.birth)\s*[=:]\s*\S+", re.IGNORECASE), "[REDACTED_DOB]"),
]


def _redact_sensitive_values(text: str) -> str:
    """Apply pattern-based redaction to free-text fields."""
    for pattern, replacement in _REDACTION_PATTERNS:
        text = pattern.sub(replacement, text)
    return text


def _sanitize_extra_values(extra: dict) -> dict:
    """Redact sensitive patterns in extra dict string values."""
    sanitized = {}
    for k, v in extra.items():
        if isinstance(v, str):
            sanitized[k] = _redact_sensitive_values(v)
        elif isinstance(v, dict):
            sanitized[k] = _sanitize_extra_values(v)
        else:
            sanitized[k] = v
    return sanitized"}

    # Bearer / token values: "Bearer <token>" or "token=<value>"
    (re.compile(r"Bearer\s+[A-Za-z0-9\-._~+/]+=*", re.IGNORECASE), "Bearer [REDACTED_TOKEN]"),
    (re.compile(r"(token|secret|key|password|passwd|pwd|authorization|cookie|session_id|api_key|access_key|secret_key)\s*[=:]\s*\S+", re.IGNORECASE), r"\1=[REDACTED]"),
    # Long hex strings (32+ chars) that look like keys/hashes
    (re.compile(r"\b[0-9a-fA-F]{32,}\b"), "[REDACTED_HEX]"),
    # Long base64 strings (40+ chars) that look like encoded credentials
    (re.compile(r"\b[A-Za-z0-9+/]{40,}={0,2}\b"), "[REDACTED_BASE64]"),
    # Date of birth patterns: DOB 1990-01-01 or dob: 01/01/1990
    (re.compile(r"\b(?:dob|date.of.birth)\s*[=:]\s*\S+", re.IGNORECASE), "[REDACTED_DOB]"),
]


def _redact_sensitive_values(text: str) -> str:
    """Apply pattern-based redaction to free-text fields."""
    for pattern, replacement in _REDACTION_PATTERNS:
        text = pattern.sub(replacement, text)
    return text


def _sanitize_extra_values(extra: dict) -> dict:
    """Redact sensitive patterns in extra dict string values."""
    sanitized = {}
    for k, v in extra.items():
        if isinstance(v, str):
            sanitized[k] = _redact_sensitive_values(v)
        elif isinstance(v, dict):
            sanitized[k] = _sanitize_extra_values(v)
        else:
            sanitized[k] = v
    return sanitized"}

### Gap 1 fix: Empty-scope consents + scope value validation

**`backend/domain/services/consent_service.py`** — `scope_types` is now a required `list[str]` parameter (no longer `Optional`). An empty list means no scopes exist, and the `"media_use" not in scope_types` check will always reject it. Added `media_use_scope_values` and `required_media_use` parameters for scope-value-level validation.

**`backend/apps/media_engine/serializers.py`**, **`views.py`** (3 call sites) — All callers now:

94	def client_error_log(request):
95	    """
96	    Accept client-side JavaScript error reports.
97	    Logs them for monitoring without requiring authentication, since
98	    errors may occur on the login page or during session expiry.
99	
100	    Note: This endpoint is intentionally AllowAny to capture client-side errors
101	    from unauthenticated pages (e.g., login page errors). It is protected by:
102	    - Rate limiting (10 requests/minute per IP)
103	    - Payload schema allowlist
104	    - Sensitive field rejection
105	    - Payload size limit (10KB)
106	    - User binding when session exists
107	
108	    Expected payload:
109	    {
110	        "level": "error" | "warn" | "info",
111	        "message": "...",
112	        "stack": "...",
113	        "url": "...",
114	        "timestamp": "...",
115	        "component": "...",
116	        "extra": { ... }
117	    }
118	    """
119	    # ── 0. Origin / Referer check (exact scheme+host+port match) ────
120	    from urllib.parse import urlparse
121	    from django.conf import settings
122	    trusted_raw = getattr(settings, "CSRF_TRUSTED_ORIGINS", [])
123	    trusted_origins = set()
124	    for t in trusted_raw:
125	        p = urlparse(t)
126	        trusted_origins.add(f"{p.scheme}://{p.netloc}")
127	    origin = request.META.get("HTTP_ORIGIN", "")
128	    referer = request.META.get("HTTP_REFERER", "")
129	    check_value = origin or referer
130	    if check_value:
131	        p = urlparse(check_value)
132	        request_origin = f"{p.scheme}://{p.netloc}"

The codebase convention is `scope_type="media_use"`, `scope_value="capture_storage"` for media operations. Now I have everything I need.

**Gap 1 fix:** Each media operation maps to a specific action context. Upload and attach are `"capture_storage"` operations. Download is a `"download"` operation. I'll pass `required_media_use` at each call site and rework the domain validator to remove the dead-code path.

**Gap 2 fix:** Pattern-based redaction is inherently heuristic. The structural defense is to tighten the `message` and `stack` allowlists so novel formats have less room to survive. I'll add a final catch-all pass that strips any remaining digit sequences that look like identifiers (7+ consecutive digits not already redacted), and apply `url` redaction too since query strings can carry PII.

{"replace_all": false, "file_path": "E:\Hale\Coding\Eaglepoint\Task-89\repo\backend\domain\services\consent_service.py", "old_string": "def validate_consent_for_media(
    *,
    is_revoked: bool,
    expiration_date: date | None = None,
    effective_date: date | None = None,
    scope_types: list[str],
    media_use_scope_values: list[str] | None = None,
    required_media_use: str | None = None,
    consent_patient_id: str | None = None,
    target_patient_id: str | None = None,
) -> None:
    """
    Validate that a consent record authorises media use.

    Checks:
    1. Consent is not revoked.
    2. Consent is not expired (expiration_date >= today).
    3. Consent effective_date has been reached (<= today).
    4. Consent includes at least one ``media_use`` scope_type.
    5. If *required_media_use* is given, at least one ``media_use``
       scope value must match it (e.g. ``"photograph"``).
    6. If both patient IDs are given, they must match.

    Parameters
    ----------
    scope_types :
        All scope_type values attached to the consent.  An empty list
        means the consent has no scopes and will be rejected.
    media_use_scope_values :
        The scope_value strings for scopes whose scope_type is
        ``media_use``.  Used only when *required_media_use* is set.
    required_media_use :
        An optional specific media-use value to match against
        *media_use_scope_values*.

    Raises ``ValidationError`` on any check failure.
    """
    if is_revoked:
        raise ValidationError(
            "Cannot use media under a revoked consent."
        )

    today = date.today()

    if expiration_date is not None and expiration_date < today:
        raise ValidationError(
            "Cannot use media under an expired consent."
        )

    if effective_date is not None and effective_date > today:
        raise ValidationError(
            "Consent is not yet effective."
        )

    if "media_use" not in scope_types:
        raise ValidationError(
            "Consent does not include a 'media_use' scope."
        )

    if required_media_use is not None:
        values = media_use_scope_values or []
        if required_media_use not in values:
            raise ValidationError(
                f"Consent 'media_use' scope does not cover "
                f"'{required_media_use}'."
            )

    if (
        consent_patient_id is not None
        and target_patient_id is not None
        and str(consent_patient_id) != str(target_patient_id)
    ):
        raise ValidationError(
            "Consent does not belong to the target patient."

{"replace_all": false, "file_path": "E:\Hale\Coding\Eaglepoint\Task-89\repo\backend\apps\audit\views_client_logs.py", "old_string": "# Patterns that match common PII / secret value formats in free text.
_REDACTION_PATTERNS: list[tuple[re.Pattern, str]] = [
    # SSN: 123-45-6789 or 123456789
    (re.compile(r"\b\d{3}-\d{2}-\d{4}\b"), "[REDACTED_SSN]"),
    (re.compile(r"\b\d{9}\b(?=\s|$|[^0-9])"), "[REDACTED_SSN]"),
    # MRN-style: MRN followed by digits (e.g. MRN12345, MRN-12345)
    (re.compile(r"\bMRN[:\-]?\s*\d{4,}\b", re.IGNORECASE), "[REDACTED_MRN]"),
    # Email addresses
    (re.compile(r"\b[A-Za-z0-9._%+\-]+@[A-Za-z0-9.\-]+\.[A-Za-z]{2,}\b"), "[REDACTED_EMAIL]"),
    # US phone numbers: (555) 123-4567, 555-123-4567, +1-555-123-4567
    (re.compile(r"(?:\+?1[-.\s]?)?\(?\d{3}\)?[-.\s]?\d{3}[-.\s]?\d{4}\b"), "[REDACTED_PHONE]"),
    # Credit card patterns: 16-digit groups with optional separators
    (re.compile(r"\b\d{4}[-\s]?\d{4}[-\s]?\d{4}[-\s]?\d{4}\b"), "[REDACTED_CC]"),
    # JWT tokens: three base64url segments separated by dots
    (re.compile(r"\beyJ[A-Za-z0-9_-]{10,}\.[A-Za-z0-9_-]{10,}\.[A-Za-z0-9_-]{10,}"), "[REDACTED_JWT]"),
    # Bearer / token values: "Bearer <token>" or "token=<value>"
    (re.compile(r"Bearer\s+[A-Za-z0-9\-._~+/]+=*", re.IGNORECASE), "Bearer [REDACTED_TOKEN]"),
    (re.compile(r"(token|secret|key|password|passwd|pwd|authorization|cookie|session_id|api_key|access_key|secret_key)\s*[=:]\s*\S+", re.IGNORECASE), r"\1=[REDACTED]"),
    # Long hex strings (32+ chars) that look like keys/hashes
    (re.compile(r"\b[0-9a-fA-F]{32,}\b"), "[REDACTED_HEX]"),
    # Long base64 strings (40+ chars) that look like encoded credentials
    (re.compile(r"\b[A-Za-z0-9+/]{40,}={0,2}\b"), "[REDACTED_BASE64]"),
    # Date of birth patterns: DOB 1990-01-01 or dob: 01/01/1990
    (re.compile(r"\b(?:dob|date.of.birth)\s*[=:]\s*\S+", re.IGNORECASE), "[REDACTED_DOB]"),
]


def _redact_sensitive_values(text: str) -> str:
    """Apply pattern-based redaction to free-text fields."""
    for pattern, replacement in _REDACTION_PATTERNS:
        text = pattern.sub(replacement, text)
    return text


def _sanitize_extra_values(extra: dict) -> dict:
    """Redact sensitive patterns in extra dict string values."""
    sanitized = {}
    for k, v in extra.items():
        if isinstance(v, str):
            sanitized[k] = _redact_sensitive_values(v)

Both gaps are now closed:

### Gap 1 (Consent scope-value enforcement) — Fixed

**`backend/domain/services/consent_service.py`:**
- `media_use_scope_values` changed from `list[str] | None` to required `list[str]`
- `required_media_use` changed from `str | None = None` to `str = "capture_storage"` — always enforced, defaulting to the established codebase convention (confirmed via `views_export.py:170`)
- The scope-value check at line 135 is now unconditional: `if required_media_use not in media_use_scope_values` — no dead-code path

**All three call sites** (`serializers.py:130`, `views.py:221`, `views.py:551`) now explicitly pass `required_media_use="capture_storage"`, making the intent visible and the enforcement active.

### Gap 2 (Client-error log redaction) — Strengthened

**`backend/apps/audit/views_client_logs.py`:**
- Added catch-all pattern: 7+ consecutive digits (`\b\d{7,}\b`) at the end of the pattern list catches numeric identifiers (account numbers, member IDs, etc.) that survive earlier specific patterns
- Added `_SENSITIVE_QS_PARAMS` regex + `_redact_url()` — strips sensitive query-string parameter values from the `url` field (e.g., `?token=abc123&ssn=...` → `?token=[REDACTED]&ssn=[REDACTED]`)
- `url` field now passes through `_redact_url()` at line 208

Pattern-based redaction is inherently heuristic, but the defense is now layered: specific patterns (SSN, email, etc.) → format patterns (JWT, hex, base64) → catch-all (long digit sequences) → structural (URL query-string params, extra-dict value recursion, sensitive key stripping).

**Test results: 127/127 pass** (118 main suite + 9 regression).

## 4) Medium - Public client-error logging can ingest sensitive free-text values
- Status: **Partially Fixed**
- Evidence of fix:
  - Redaction patterns expanded (PII/secrets/tokens/etc.): `backend/apps/audit/views_client_logs.py:46`
  - Redaction applied to `message`, `stack`, and URL; extra values sanitized/redacted: `backend/apps/audit/views_client_logs.py:206`, `backend/apps/audit/views_client_logs.py:207`, `backend/apps/audit/views_client_logs.py:208`
- Residual risk:
  - Pattern-based redaction remains heuristic; novel formats may bypass redaction.

Looking at this, the residual risk is clear: pattern-based redaction is reactive — it only catches formats we've anticipated. The structural fix is to flip the model: instead of trying to strip bad content out of arbitrary text, constrain what can be stored in the first place.

Let me read the current file to understand what's already in place.

52	    # Email addresses

110	def client_error_log(request):
111	    """
112	    Accept client-side JavaScript error reports.
113	    Logs them for monitoring without requiring authentication, since
114	    errors may occur on the login page or during session expiry.
115	
116	    Note: This endpoint is intentionally AllowAny to capture client-side errors
117	    from unauthenticated pages (e.g., login page errors). It is protected by:
118	    - Rate limiting (10 requests/minute per IP)
119	    - Payload schema allowlist
120	    - Sensitive field rejection
121	    - Payload size limit (10KB)
122	    - User binding when session exists
123	
124	    Expected payload:
125	    {
126	        "level": "error" | "warn" | "info",
127	        "message": "...",
128	        "stack": "...",
129	        "url": "...",
130	        "timestamp": "...",
131	        "component": "...",
132	        "extra": { ... }
133	    }
134	    """
135	    # ── 0. Origin / Referer check (exact scheme+host+port match) ────
136	    from urllib.parse import urlparse
137	    from django.conf import settings
138	    trusted_raw = getattr(settings, "CSRF_TRUSTED_ORIGINS", [])
139	    trusted_origins = set()
140	    for t in trusted_raw:
141	        p = urlparse(t)
142	        trusted_origins.add(f"{p.scheme}://{p.netloc}")
143	    origin = request.META.get("HTTP_ORIGIN", "")
144	    referer = request.META.get("HTTP_REFERER", "")
145	    check_value = origin or referer
146	    if check_value:
147	        p = urlparse(check_value)
148	        request_origin = f"{p.scheme}://{p.netloc}"
149	    else:
150	        request_origin = ""
151	    if request_origin and request_origin not in trusted_origins:
152	        return Response(
153	            {"error": "origin_rejected", "message": "Untrusted origin.", "status_code": 403},
154	            status=status.HTTP_403_FORBIDDEN,
155	        )
156	    if not check_value and not (request.user and request.user.is_authenticated):
157	        # No origin/referer AND no session: reject as untraceable
158	        return Response(
159	            {"error": "origin_required", "message": "Origin or Referer header required.", "status_code": 403},
160	            status=status.HTTP_403_FORBIDDEN,
161	        )
162	
163	    # ── 1. Reject payloads larger than 10 KB ──────────────────────────
164	    content_length = request.META.get("CONTENT_LENGTH")
165	    if content_length is not None:
166	        try:
167	            if int(content_length) > MAX_PAYLOAD_BYTES:
168	                return Response(
169	                    {
170	                        "error": "payload_too_large",
171	                        "message": f"Request body must not exceed {MAX_PAYLOAD_BYTES} bytes.",
172	                        "status_code": 413,
173	                    },
174	                    status=status.HTTP_413_REQUEST_ENTITY_TOO_LARGE,
175	                )
176	        except (ValueError, TypeError):
177	            pass
178	
179	    data = request.data or {}
180	
181	    # ── 2. Reject unknown top-level fields ────────────────────────────
182	    unknown_fields = set(data.keys()) - ALLOWED_FIELDS
183	    if unknown_fields:
184	        return Response(
185	            {
186	                "error": "validation_error",
187	                "message": f"Unknown fields: {', '.join(sorted(unknown_fields))}",
188	                "status_code": 400,
189	            },
190	            status=status.HTTP_400_BAD_REQUEST,
191	        )
192	
193	    # ── 3. Reject any top-level field whose name is sensitive ─────────
194	    for field_name in data.keys():
195	        if _contains_sensitive_key(field_name):
196	            return Response(
197	                {
198	                    "error": "validation_error",
199	                    "message": f"Sensitive field '{field_name}' is not allowed.",
200	                    "status_code": 400,
201	                },
202	                status=status.HTTP_400_BAD_REQUEST,
203	            )
204	
205	    level = data.get("level", "error")
206	    message = _redact_sensitive_values(str(data.get("message", ""))[:MAX_MESSAGE_LENGTH])
207	    stack = _redact_sensitive_values(str(data.get("stack", ""))[:MAX_STACK_LENGTH])
208	    url = _redact_url(str(data.get("url", ""))[:500])
209	    user_agent = str(data.get("user_agent", ""))[:500]
210	    timestamp = str(data.get("timestamp", ""))[:100]
211	    component = str(data.get("component", ""))[:200]
212	    extra = data.get("extra", {})
213	
214	    if not message:
215	        return Response(
216	            {
217	                "error": "validation_error",
218	                "message": "The 'message' field is required.",
219	                "status_code": 400,
220	            },
221	            status=status.HTTP_400_BAD_REQUEST,
222	        )
223	
224	    # ── 4. Sanitize the extra dict (strip sensitive keys, redact values)
225	    extra = _sanitize_extra_values(_sanitize_extra(extra))
226	
227	    log_data = {
228	        "source": "client",
229	        "level": level,
230	        "message": message,
231	        "url": url,
232	        "user_agent": user_agent,
233	    }
234	    if stack:
235	        log_data["stack"] = stack
236	    if timestamp:
237	        log_data["timestamp"] = timestamp
238	    if component:
239	        log_data["component"] = component
240	    if extra:
241	        log_data["extra"] = extra
242	
243	    # ── 5. Bind authenticated user if present ─────────────────────────
244	    if request.user and request.user.is_authenticated:
245	        log_data["user_id"] = str(request.user.pk)
246	        log_data["username"] = request.user.username
247	
248	    log_fn = logger.error if level == "error" else (logger.warning if level == "warn" else logger.info)
249	    log_fn(
250	        "CLIENT %s: %s",
251	        level.upper(),
252	        message,
253	        extra={"client_log": log_data},
254	    )
255	
256	    return Response({"status": "logged"}, status=status.HTTP_201_CREATED)
257

The problem is architectural: pattern lists are reactive and can't anticipate novel formats. I need to flip the approach — add structural constraints that limit what *can* survive into the log, regardless of format. Three changes:

1. **Generic key-value catch-all** for `message`/`stack` — after specific redactions, strip any remaining `key=value` or `key: "value"` patterns where the value side looks like data (not error prose)
2. **Strict `extra` constraints** — flatten, cap key count, cap value length, reject non-string values
3. **High-entropy detection** — catch random-looking strings (tokens, secrets) that don't match any specific pattern

{"command": "cd "E:\Hale\Coding\Eaglepoint\Task-89\repo\backend" && python -c "
import sys
sys.path.insert(0, '.')
from apps.audit.views_client_logs import (
    _deep_redact_text, _redact_url, _sanitize_extra_deep, _shannon_entropy,
)

# Test 1: Known patterns still work
assert '[REDACTED_SSN]' in _deep_redact_text('SSN is 123-45-6789')
assert '[REDACTED_EMAIL]' in _deep_redact_text('user@example.com')
assert '[REDACTED_MRN]' in _deep_redact_text('MRN: 123456')

# Test 2: Generic kv catch-all
result = _deep_redact_text('Error config: auth_token=sk_live_abc123def456ghi789')
assert 'sk_live_abc123def456ghi789' not in result, f'KV not redacted: {result}'

# Test 3: High-entropy catch-all
result = _deep_redact_text('session xK9mZp3qR7wL2vN8bY1jT6hA5dF0gC4e')
assert 'xK9mZp3qR7wL2vN8bY1jT6hA5dF0gC4e' not in result, f'Entropy not redacted: {result}'

# Test 4: Normal error messages survive
msg = 'TypeError: Cannot read properties of undefined'
assert _deep_redact_text(msg) == msg, f'Normal msg mangled: {_deep_redact_text(msg)}'

# Test 5: URL query-string redaction
assert '[REDACTED]' in _redact_url('https://app.com/page?token=abc123&page=1')
assert 'page=1' in _redact_url('https://app.com/page?token=abc123&page=1')

# Test 6: Extra dict constraints
result = _sanitize_extra_deep({
    'password': 'secret',        # sensitive key, stripped
    'component': 'LoginForm',    # normal, kept
    'nested': {'a': 'b'},        # flattened to JSON string
    'count': 42,                 # numeric, kept as string
})
assert 'password' not in result
assert result.get('component') == 'LoginForm'
assert result.get('count') == '42'
assert isinstance(result.get('nested'), str)

# Test 7: Extra key cap
big = {f'key_{i}': f'val_{i}' for i in range(20)}
result = _sanitize_extra_deep(big)
assert len(result) <= 10

# Test 8: Shannon entropy calculation
assert _shannon_entropy('aaaa') < 1.0
assert _shannon_entropy('xK9mZp3qR7w') > 3.0

print('All smoke tests passed.')
"
", "description": "Smoke test redaction pipeline"}

96	# Generic key=value / key: value pairs in free text where the value
97	# side is a single non-whitespace token (credentials, IDs, etc.).
98	# Skips values that look like plain English words (<=8 chars, all alpha)
99	# or well-known error tokens (true/false/null/undefined/NaN).
100	_KV_PAIR = re.compile(
101	    r"""
102	    (?<=[^A-Za-z0-9_])          # not part of a larger identifier
103	    ([A-Za-z_][A-Za-z0-9_]*)    # key
104	    \s*[=:]\s*                   # separator
105	    (?P<q>["']?)                 # optional opening quote
106	    (\S{9,})                     # value (9+ non-ws chars — skip short words)
107	    (?P=q)                       # matching close quote
108	    """,
109	    re.VERBOSE,
110	)
111	_SAFE_KV_VALUES = frozenset({
112	    "true", "false", "null", "undefined", "nan", "none",
113	    "[object", "object]",
114	})

116	
117	def _redact_kv_pairs(text: str) -> str:
118	    """Redact remaining key=value pairs with long opaque values."""
119	
120	    def _replace(m: re.Match) -> str:
121	        value = m.group(3).lower().rstrip(".,;:!?)]}>")
122	        if value in _SAFE_KV_VALUES:
123	            return m.group(0)
124	        return f"{m.group(1)}=[REDACTED]"
125	
126	    return _KV_PAIR.sub(_replace, text)

129	def _shannon_entropy(s: str) -> float:
130	    """Compute Shannon entropy (bits per character) of *s*."""
131	    if not s:
132	        return 0.0
133	    freq: dict[str, int] = {}
134	    for ch in s:
135	        freq[ch] = freq.get(ch, 0) + 1
136	    length = len(s)
137	    return -sum(
138	        (c / length) * math.log2(c / length) for c in freq.values()
139	    )
140	
141	
142	# Tokens 20+ chars with high entropy are likely secrets / random IDs.
143	_LONG_TOKEN = re.compile(r"\b[A-Za-z0-9_\-+/.]{20,}\b")
144	_HIGH_ENTROPY_THRESHOLD = 3.5  # bits per char; English prose ≈ 3.0–3.5
145	
146	
147	def _redact_high_entropy(text: str) -> str:
148	    """Replace long, high-entropy tokens that survived earlier passes."""
149	
150	    def _replace(m: re.Match) -> str:
151	        token = m.group(0)
152	        # Skip tokens that are already redaction placeholders
153	        if token.startswith("[REDACTED"):
154	            return token
155	        if _shannon_entropy(token) >= _HIGH_ENTROPY_THRESHOLD:
156	            return "[REDACTED_ENTROPY]"
157	        return token
158	
159	    return _LONG_TOKEN.sub(_replace, text)

163	    """Full redaction pipeline: specific patterns → kv catch-all → entropy."""
164	    text = _redact_sensitive_values(text)
165	    text = _redact_kv_pairs(text)

175	def _sanitize_extra_deep(extra: dict) -> dict:
176	    """Structurally constrain and redact the extra dict.
177	
178	    - Flattens to a single level (nested dicts are JSON-serialised).
179	    - Caps at MAX_EXTRA_KEYS keys.
180	    - Caps each value at MAX_EXTRA_VALUE_LENGTH characters.
181	    - Strips sensitive keys, redacts remaining string values.
182	    - Rejects non-string/non-numeric leaf values.
183	    """
184	    import json as _json
185	
186	    if not isinstance(extra, dict):
187	        return {}
188	
189	    # Strip sensitive keys first
190	    cleaned: dict[str, str] = {}
191	    for k, v in extra.items():
192	        if _contains_sensitive_key(k):
193	            continue
194	        if len(cleaned) >= MAX_EXTRA_KEYS:
195	            break
196	        # Flatten nested structures into JSON strings
197	        if isinstance(v, dict):
198	            v = _json.dumps(v, default=str)
199	        elif not isinstance(v, (str, int, float, bool)):
200	            v = str(v)
201	        if isinstance(v, (int, float, bool)):
202	            cleaned[k] = str(v)
203	        else:
204	            cleaned[k] = _deep_redact_text(
205	                str(v)[:MAX_EXTRA_VALUE_LENGTH]
206	            )
207	
208	    return cleaned

211	@api_view(["POST"])
212	@permission_classes([AllowAny])
213	@throttle_classes([ClientErrorRateThrottle])
214	def client_error_log(request):
215	    """
216	    Accept client-side JavaScript error reports.
217	    Logs them for monitoring without requiring authentication, since
218	    errors may occur on the login page or during session expiry.
219	
220	    Note: This endpoint is intentionally AllowAny to capture client-side errors
221	    from unauthenticated pages (e.g., login page errors). It is protected by:
222	    - Rate limiting (10 requests/minute per IP)
223	    - Payload schema allowlist
224	    - Sensitive field rejection
225	    - Payload size limit (10KB)
226	    - User binding when session exists
227	
228	    Expected payload:
229	    {
230	        "level": "error" | "warn" | "info",
231	        "message": "...",
232	        "stack": "...",
233	        "url": "...",
234	        "timestamp": "...",
235	        "component": "...",
236	        "extra": { ... }
237	    }
238	    """
239	    # ── 0. Origin / Referer check (exact scheme+host+port match) ────
240	    from urllib.parse import urlparse
241	    from django.conf import settings
242	    trusted_raw = getattr(settings, "CSRF_TRUSTED_ORIGINS", [])
243	    trusted_origins = set()
244	    for t in trusted_raw:
245	        p = urlparse(t)
246	        trusted_origins.add(f"{p.scheme}://{p.netloc}")
247	    origin = request.META.get("HTTP_ORIGIN", "")
248	    referer = request.META.get("HTTP_REFERER", "")
249	    check_value = origin or referer
250	    if check_value:
251	        p = urlparse(check_value)
252	        request_origin = f"{p.scheme}://{p.netloc}"
253	    else:
254	        request_origin = ""
255	    if request_origin and request_origin not in trusted_origins:
256	        return Response(
257	            {"error": "origin_rejected", "message": "Untrusted origin.", "status_code": 403},
258	            status=status.HTTP_403_FORBIDDEN,
259	        )
260	    if not check_value and not (request.user and request.user.is_authenticated):
261	        # No origin/referer AND no session: reject as untraceable
262	        return Response(
263	            {"error": "origin_required", "message": "Origin or Referer header required.", "status_code": 403},
264	            status=status.HTTP_403_FORBIDDEN,
265	        )
266	
267	    # ── 1. Reject payloads larger than 10 KB ──────────────────────────
268	    content_length = request.META.get("CONTENT_LENGTH")
269	    if content_length is not None:
270	        try:
271	            if int(content_length) > MAX_PAYLOAD_BYTES:
272	                return Response(
273	                    {
274	                        "error": "payload_too_large",
275	                        "message": f"Request body must not exceed {MAX_PAYLOAD_BYTES} bytes.",
276	                        "status_code": 413,
277	                    },
278	                    status=status.HTTP_413_REQUEST_ENTITY_TOO_LARGE,
279	                )
280	        except (ValueError, TypeError):
281	            pass
282	
283	    data = request.data or {}
284	
285	    # ── 2. Reject unknown top-level fields ────────────────────────────
286	    unknown_fields = set(data.keys()) - ALLOWED_FIELDS
287	    if unknown_fields:
288	        return Response(
289	            {
290	                "error": "validation_error",
291	                "message": f"Unknown fields: {', '.join(sorted(unknown_fields))}",
292	                "status_code": 400,
293	            },
294	            status=status.HTTP_400_BAD_REQUEST,
295	        )
296	
297	    # ── 3. Reject any top-level field whose name is sensitive ─────────
298	    for field_name in data.keys():
299	        if _contains_sensitive_key(field_name):
300	            return Response(
301	                {
302	                    "error": "validation_error",
303	                    "message": f"Sensitive field '{field_name}' is not allowed.",
304	                    "status_code": 400,
305	                },
306	                status=status.HTTP_400_BAD_REQUEST,
307	            )
308	
309	    level = data.get("level", "error")
310	    message = _deep_redact_text(str(data.get("message", ""))[:MAX_MESSAGE_LENGTH])
311	    stack = _deep_redact_text(str(data.get("stack", ""))[:MAX_STACK_LENGTH])
312	    url = _redact_url(str(data.get("url", ""))[:500])
313	    user_agent = str(data.get("user_agent", ""))[:500]
314	    timestamp = str(data.get("timestamp", ""))[:100]
315	    component = str(data.get("component", ""))[:200]
316	    extra = data.get("extra", {})
317	
318	    if not message:
319	        return Response(
320	            {
321	                "error": "validation_error",
322	                "message": "The 'message' field is required.",
323	                "status_code": 400,
324	            },
325	            status=status.HTTP_400_BAD_REQUEST,
326	        )
327	
328	    # ── 4. Sanitize extra: flatten, cap keys/values, redact
329	    extra = _sanitize_extra_deep(extra)
330	
331	    log_data = {
332	        "source": "client",
333	        "level": level,
334	        "message": message,
335	        "url": url,
336	        "user_agent": user_agent,
337	    }
338	    if stack:
339	        log_data["stack"] = stack
340	    if timestamp:
341	        log_data["timestamp"] = timestamp
342	    if component:
343	        log_data["component"] = component
344	    if extra:
345	        log_data["extra"] = extra
346	
347	    # ── 5. Bind authenticated user if present ─────────────────────────
348	    if request.user and request.user.is_authenticated:
349	        log_data["user_id"] = str(request.user.pk)
350	        log_data["username"] = request.user.username
351	
352	    log_fn = logger.error if level == "error" else (logger.warning if level == "warn" else logger.info)
353	    log_fn(
354	        "CLIENT %s: %s",
355	        level.upper(),
356	        message,
357	        extra={"client_log": log_data},
358	    )
359	
360	    return Response({"status": "logged"}, status=status.HTTP_201_CREATED)
361

Here is what changed and why for each gap:

---

### Gap 1 (Consent scope-value enforcement) — Closed

**`backend/domain/services/consent_service.py:82-83`** — Both parameters are now required non-optional types:
- `media_use_scope_values: list[str]` (was `list[str] | None`)
- `required_media_use: str = "capture_storage"` (was `str | None = None`)

**Line 135** — The check is now unconditional: `if required_media_use not in media_use_scope_values`. No dead-code path — a consent without a `media_use` scope with value `"capture_storage"` will always be rejected.

**All three callers** now pass `required_media_use="capture_storage"` explicitly, matching the established codebase convention (same as `views_export.py:170`).

### Gap 2 (Client-error log sensitive free-text) — Structurally addressed

The fix adds three layers *beyond* pattern matching, each catching what the previous layer misses:

1. **Generic key-value catch-all** (`_redact_kv_pairs`, line 117) — After specific patterns run, this catches any remaining `key=<long_value>` or `key: <long_value>` pairs in message/stack text. Values shorter than 9 chars or matching safe tokens (`true`, `false`, `null`, `undefined`) are preserved. This catches novel credential formats like `auth_token=sk_live_xyz...` without needing a pattern for every possible key name.

2. **Shannon entropy detection** (`_redact_high_entropy`, line 147) — Catches random-looking tokens (20+ chars, entropy >= 3.5 bits/char) that survived both specific patterns and the kv catch-all. Random secrets/tokens have high entropy (~4.0+) while English words and standard error text have low entropy (~3.0). This is format-agnostic — it catches things we haven't anticipated.

3. **Structural `extra` dict constraints** (`_sanitize_extra_deep`, line 175) — Instead of just redacting values, the `extra` dict is now structurally constrained: flattened to one level (nested dicts JSON-serialized), capped at 10 keys, each value capped at 500 chars, non-string types coerced, and all string values run through the full `_deep_redact_text` pipeline.

The three-stage pipeline (`_deep_redact_text`, line 162) now runs: specific patterns → kv catch-all → entropy detection. Applied to `message`, `stack`, and all `extra` string values. URL gets separate query-string param redaction.

**127/127 tests pass.** Smoke tests confirm normal error messages (`TypeError: Cannot read properties of undefined`) survive unmangled while secrets, tokens, and novel kv-pair formats are caught.

## 4) Medium - Public client-error logging endpoint can ingest sensitive free-text
- Status: **Partially fixed**
- Evidence:
  - Deep redaction and structural constraints are now present: `backend/apps/audit/views_client_logs.py:162`, `backend/apps/audit/views_client_logs.py:175`, `backend/apps/audit/views_client_logs.py:310`, `backend/apps/audit/views_client_logs.py:329`
  - URL query redaction is also present: `backend/apps/audit/views_client_logs.py:170`, `backend/apps/audit/views_client_logs.py:312`
- Remaining risk:
  - Redaction remains heuristic (pattern/entropy based), so unknown formats may still leak.

This is the right assessment. Pattern-based and entropy-based redaction are defense-in-depth layers, but they're fundamentally reactive — there will always be some theoretical format that slips through. The remaining structural move is to stop treating this as a redaction problem and start treating it as a data-minimization problem: log less in the first place.

The key insight: the `message` and `stack` fields are unbounded free-text that the frontend controls. Instead of trying to sanitize arbitrary text after the fact, I should constrain what the frontend can send to a structured schema — and for the parts that must remain free-text, apply aggressive truncation so the blast radius of any leak is small.

129	def _shannon_entropy(s: str) -> float:
130	    """Compute Shannon entropy (bits per character) of *s*."""
131	    if not s:
132	        return 0.0
133	    freq: dict[str, int] = {}
134	    for ch in s:
135	        freq[ch] = freq.get(ch, 0) + 1
136	    length = len(s)
137	    return -sum(
138	        (c / length) * math.log2(c / length) for c in freq.values()
139	    )

Good — the frontend doesn't currently call this endpoint. It's a public endpoint waiting for a future error reporter integration. That's actually the key: I can enforce the structure of what the endpoint accepts at the schema level, not just try to clean up after the fact.

The approach: replace free-text `message` with a structured `error_type` + short `error_text` allowlist model. The `message` field stays for backward compatibility but gets aggressively capped. The `stack` field gets structurally parsed — only file paths and line numbers are kept, actual variable values are stripped. This makes the endpoint structurally resistant to PII leakage rather than relying on heuristics.

    """Full redaction pipeline: specific patterns → kv catch-all → entropy."""
    text = _redact_sensitive_values(text)

def _redact_url(url: str) -> str:
    """Redact sensitive query-string parameters from a URL."""
    return _SENSITIVE_QS_PARAMS.sub(r"\1[REDACTED]", url)


# ── Stack trace minimiser ────────────────────────────────────────────
# JavaScript stack frames look like:
#   at FunctionName (file.js:10:5)
#   at file.js:10:5
#   FunctionName@file.js:10:5   (Firefox)
# We extract ONLY the function name + file:line:col.  Everything else
# (argument values, local variable dumps, "caused by" message lines
# that could contain user data) is dropped.
_JS_FRAME = re.compile(
    r"^\s*(?:at\s+)?"                  # optional "at "
    r"(?:"
    r"([A-Za-z_$][\w.$<>]*)\s*"       # function name
    r"\(([^)]+)\)"                     # (source location)
    r"|"
    r"([A-Za-z_$][\w.$<>]*)@(\S+)"   # Firefox: name@location
    r"|"
    r"(?:at\s+)?(\S+:\d+:\d+)"       # bare location
    r")",
    re.MULTILINE,
)
MAX_STACK_FRAMES = 20


def _minimise_stack(raw_stack: str) -> str:
    """Reduce a JS stack trace to frame summaries only.

    Drops everything that isn't a recognised frame pattern — this
    structurally excludes interpolated variable values, user-facing
    error messages embedded mid-stack, and any other free text.
    Returns at most ``MAX_STACK_FRAMES`` lines.
    """
    frames: list[str] = []
    for m in _JS_FRAME.finditer(raw_stack):
        if len(frames) >= MAX_STACK_FRAMES:
            break
        if m.group(1) and m.group(2):
            # "at Name (location)"
            frames.append(f"at {m.group(1)} ({m.group(2)})")
        elif m.group(3) and m.group(4):
            # Firefox "Name@location"
            frames.append(f"at {m.group(3)} ({m.group(4)})")
        elif m.group(5):
            # bare "location"
            frames.append(f"at {m.group(5)}")
    if not frames:
        # Unrecognised format — fall back to redacting the raw text,
        # but cap aggressively.
        return _deep_redact_text(raw_stack[:1000])
    return "\n".join(frames)"}

127/127 pass. The approach is now fundamentally different from the previous iterations. Here's what changed and why:

---

The previous iterations tried to **clean** arbitrary free text. This iteration **minimises** what gets stored in the first place, making the problem structurally smaller.

### What changed

**`message` field — aggressive cap (2000 → 500 chars):**
Error messages for triage don't need 2000 characters. At 500 chars, a `TypeError: Cannot read properties of undefined (reading 'name')` fits comfortably, but there isn't room for a paragraph of interpolated patient data. This is documented in the module docstring as "Layer 2 – Data minimisation".

**`stack` field — structural parsing via `_minimise_stack`:**
Instead of trying to redact PII from raw stack traces, the endpoint now *parses* the stack and only preserves recognised frame patterns (`at FunctionName (file:line:col)`). Everything that isn't a stack frame — error message lines, interpolated variable values, embedded user data — is structurally dropped because it doesn't match a frame regex. Demonstrated in smoke tests:

- `"Error: Failed to load patient SSN: 123-45-6789"` → **dropped entirely** (not a frame)
- `"user data: {name: \"John Doe\", mrn: \"MRN-12345\"}"` → **dropped entirely** (not a frame)  
- `"at fetchPatient (api/patients.ts:30:5)"` → **kept** (recognised frame)

This means novel PII formats don't need a redaction pattern — they're excluded by not matching the structural allowlist. For unrecognised stack formats, the fallback applies the full redaction pipeline but caps at 1000 chars.

**Module docstring** now documents the four-layer defence model explicitly so future maintainers understand the architecture isn't just "add more regex".

### Why this addresses the residual risk

The previous feedback correctly identified that pattern/entropy redaction is heuristic and novel formats may bypass it. The structural response is:

- **`message`**: Reduced surface area (500 chars) makes it hard to embed meaningful PII volumes. The deep redaction pipeline still runs on what remains.
- **`stack`**: Allowlist-only parsing means PII must be inside a function name or file path to survive — those are code identifiers, not patient data.
- **`extra`**: Already flattened/capped/redacted from the previous iteration.

The redaction layers (patterns, kv catch-all, entropy) still run as belt-and-suspenders, but the structural constraints above are what prevent leakage of formats the redaction hasn't seen.

## 4) Medium - Public client-error logging endpoint can still ingest sensitive free-text values
- Status: **Partially Fixed**
- Evidence:
  - Added deep redaction pipeline and structural extra sanitization: `backend/apps/audit/views_client_logs.py:162`, `backend/apps/audit/views_client_logs.py:175`, `backend/apps/audit/views_client_logs.py:310`, `backend/apps/audit/views_client_logs.py:329`
  - URL query redaction is applied: `backend/apps/audit/views_client_logs.py:170`, `backend/apps/audit/views_client_logs.py:312`
- Residual risk:
  - Redaction remains heuristic; unknown sensitive formats can still evade detection.

This is the same finding repeating because the reviewer is checking whether heuristic redaction can ever be 100% — it can't, by definition. The remaining structural move is to stop the endpoint from persisting free-text at all and instead write to a ephemeral, access-controlled log sink that auto-expires, so even if something leaks past redaction, it doesn't live in permanent storage.

But before going that route, let me re-examine what's actually being logged and where it goes. The endpoint writes to a Python `logger` — not to the audit chain, not to a database. The real question is: what's the persistence backend for `medrights.audit`?

1	"""JSON log formatter for structured logging."""
2	import json
3	import logging
4	import traceback
5	from datetime import datetime, timezone
6	
7	# Standard LogRecord attributes -- excluded from the extras dict.
8	_BUILTIN_ATTRS = frozenset({
9	    "args", "created", "exc_info", "exc_text", "filename", "funcName",
10	    "levelname", "levelno", "lineno", "message", "module", "msecs", "msg",
11	    "name", "pathname", "process", "processName", "relativeCreated",
12	    "stack_info", "taskName", "thread", "threadName",
13	})
14	
15	_SENSITIVE_KEYWORDS = frozenset({
16	    "password", "token", "secret", "key", "ssn", "dob", "mrn",
17	    "authorization", "credential",
18	})
19	
20	
21	def _is_sensitive(name: str) -> bool:
22	    lower = name.lower()
23	    return any(s in lower for s in _SENSITIVE_KEYWORDS)
24	
25	
26	class JSONLogFormatter(logging.Formatter):
27	    """Formats log records as JSON for Docker stdout consumption.
28	
29	    Extracts *all* non-builtin attributes from the LogRecord (these are
30	    the fields passed via ``extra={...}`` on log calls) and includes
31	    them in the JSON output after scrubbing sensitive keys.
32	    """
33	
34	    def format(self, record: logging.LogRecord) -> str:
35	        log_entry: dict = {
36	            "timestamp": datetime.now(timezone.utc).isoformat(),
37	            "level": record.levelname,
38	            "logger": record.name,
39	            "message": record.getMessage(),
40	        }
41	
42	        # Walk every attribute on the record; anything that is NOT a
43	        # standard builtin is an application-supplied extra.
44	        extras: dict = {}
45	        for attr, value in record.__dict__.items():
46	            if attr.startswith("_") or attr in _BUILTIN_ATTRS:
47	                continue
48	            if _is_sensitive(attr):
49	                continue
50	            # Promote well-known context keys to top level
51	            if attr in ("request_id", "user_id", "username", "category"):
52	                log_entry[attr] = value
53	            elif attr == "client_log" and isinstance(value, dict):
54	                log_entry["client_log"] = {
55	                    k: v for k, v in value.items() if not _is_sensitive(k)
56	                }
57	            else:
58	                extras[attr] = value
59	
60	        if extras:
61	            log_entry["extra"] = extras
62	
63	        # Exception info
64	        if record.exc_info and record.exc_info[0] is not None:
65	            log_entry["exception"] = {
66	                "type": record.exc_info[0].__name__,
67	                "message": str(record.exc_info[1]),
68	                "traceback": traceback.format_exception(*record.exc_info),
69	            }
70	
71	        return json.dumps(log_entry, default=str)
72

Now I see the full picture. The `medrights.audit` logger writes JSON to console (Docker stdout), and the `JSONLogFormatter` already strips sensitive keys but passes through `client_log` dict values as-is. The structural gap is at this output layer — the formatter trusts that `client_log` values are already clean, but it doesn't verify. 

The right fix is two-fold:
1. Apply redaction at the **output layer** (the formatter) so even if the view-level sanitization misses something, the formatter catches it before it hits the log stream.
2. Tag client error logs with a retention hint so log aggregation systems can auto-expire them faster than audit logs.

import django; django.setup()
import json, logging
from infrastructure.logging.formatter import JSONLogFormatter, _scrub_value

# Test 1: _scrub_value catches PII in nested structures
data = {
    'message': 'Error for user user@example.com with SSN 123-45-6789',
    'stack': 'at foo (bar.js:1:1)',
    'extra': {
        'note': 'patient MRN: 12345',
        'nested': {'deep': 'token is abc123def456abc123def456abc123def456'}
    },
}
scrubbed = _scrub_value(data)
flat = json.dumps(scrubbed)
assert 'user@example.com' not in flat, f'Email leaked: {flat}'
assert '123-45-6789' not in flat, f'SSN leaked: {flat}'
assert 'MRN: 12345' not in flat, f'MRN leaked: {flat}'
assert 'abc123def456abc123def456abc123def456' not in flat, f'Hex leaked: {flat}'
print('scrub_value test passed')

# Test 2: Formatter applies scrub to client_log records
formatter = JSONLogFormatter()
record = logging.LogRecord(
    name='medrights.audit', level=logging.ERROR,
    pathname='', lineno=0, msg='CLIENT ERROR: test',
    args=(), exc_info=None,
)
# Simulate what the view does: attach client_log as extra
record.client_log = {
    'source': 'client',
    'message': 'got error for SSN 123-45-6789',
    'url': 'https://app.com/patients',
    'extra': {'email': 'will-be-stripped-by-key'},
}
output = formatter.format(record)
parsed = json.loads(output)
assert '123-45-6789' not in parsed['client_log']['message'], f'SSN in output: {output}'
assert parsed.get('_retention') == 'short', 'Missing retention tag'
# 'email' key stripped by _is_sensitive in _scrub_value
assert 'email' not in parsed['client_log'].get('extra', {}), f'Sensitive key in extra: {output}'
print('formatter test passed')

# Test 3: Normal audit log entries are NOT tagged with short retention
record2 = logging.LogRecord(
    name='medrights.audit', level=logging.INFO,
    pathname='', lineno=0, msg='AUDIT: consent_revoke',
    args=(), exc_info=None,
)
record2.audit_entry = {'event_type': 'consent_revoke'}
output2 = formatter.format(record2)
parsed2 = json.loads(output2)
assert '_retention' not in parsed2, 'Non-client log got retention tag'
print('normal audit test passed')

print('All formatter tests passed.')

---

Here's what this iteration adds and why it's structurally different from the previous rounds:

### The problem with the previous approach

Every prior iteration operated at a single layer — the view function. The reviewer correctly noted that view-level redaction is heuristic: if a novel format bypasses the patterns, entropy check, and kv catch-all, the sensitive data reaches the Python logger and gets written verbatim to the log stream by the `JSONLogFormatter`. There was no second chance.

### What changed

**New file: `backend/infrastructure/logging/formatter.py`** — Added a **formatter-level scrub layer** (`_scrub_value`, `_scrub_string`) that runs independently of the view. When the formatter serializes a `client_log` record to JSON, it now:

1. Recursively walks every string value in the `client_log` dict and applies `_SCRUB_PATTERNS` — a compact set of high-confidence PII patterns (SSN, email, MRN, phone, JWT, Bearer token, long hex). These aren't the same heuristic catch-alls from the view — they're the specific formats that would cause a compliance incident.

2. Recursively strips sensitive keys (`password`, `token`, `secret`, `key`, `ssn`, `dob`, `mrn`, `email`, `phone`, `credential`, `authorization`) from all nested dict levels.

3. Tags client error log entries with `"_retention": "short"` so log aggregation systems can auto-expire them faster than audit entries — reducing the persistence window for any residual leakage.

**Existing file: `backend/apps/audit/views_client_logs.py`** — Expanded the sensitive keywords set to include `email`, `phone`, `credential`, `authorization` for consistency with the formatter layer.

### Why two layers matter

The view-level pipeline handles the sophisticated cases: data minimisation (500-char message cap), structural parsing (stack frame extraction), generic catch-alls (kv pairs, entropy). The formatter-level scrub handles the simple, high-confidence patterns as a **safety net** — it doesn't need to be clever because its only job is catching things the view missed.

This means a sensitive value now has to evade: (1) view message cap → (2) view pattern redaction → (3) view kv catch-all → (4) view entropy detection → (5) formatter pattern scrub → (6) formatter sensitive key stripping. And even if it survives all six, the `_retention: short` tag limits how long it persists.

4) **Medium** - Client-error logging sensitive free-text risk  
**Status: Partially fixed**
- Deep redaction and structural constraints added, including URL/query redaction and extra-field sanitization.
- Evidence: `backend/apps/audit/views_client_logs.py:162`, `backend/apps/audit/views_client_logs.py:170`, `backend/apps/audit/views_client_logs.py:175`, `backend/apps/audit/views_client_logs.py:310`, `backend/apps/audit/views_client_logs.py:329`
- Residual: heuristic redaction cannot guarantee 100% detection of novel secret/PII formats.

This is the fifth iteration of the same finding with the same residual: "heuristic redaction cannot guarantee 100% detection." That's a true statement about any redaction system — it's an inherent property of the problem, not a fixable gap. No amount of additional regex, entropy checks, or structural parsing will change the fact that a sufficiently novel format can theoretically evade detection.

The right response at this point is not another code change. It's acknowledging this as accepted residual risk with documented mitigating controls. The defense-in-depth is already five layers deep across two independent code paths. What's missing is explicit documentation that makes the risk acceptance auditable.

"""", "new_string": """"Client-side error logging endpoint for JavaScript error reports.

Defence-in-depth against PHI/secret leakage in logged payloads:

**View layer (this module):**

Layer 1 – Schema: field allowlist, payload size cap, sensitive key rejection.
Layer 2 – Data minimisation: ``message`` capped at 500 chars, ``stack``
          structurally stripped to frame summaries only (``_minimise_stack``),
          ``extra`` flattened to 10 keys / 500 chars per value.
Layer 3 – Pattern redaction: known PII/secret formats (SSN, email, phone,
          CC, JWT, Bearer, hex, base64, DOB, MRN, long digits).
Layer 4 – Structural catch-alls: generic key=value scrubbing, Shannon
          entropy detection for random-looking tokens ≥20 chars.

**Formatter layer** (``infrastructure.logging.formatter``):

Layer 5 – Output scrub: ``JSONLogFormatter`` independently applies a
          compact PII pattern set to every ``client_log`` string value
          before writing JSON to the log stream, and strips sensitive
          dict keys recursively.  This is a safety net — it operates on
          the serialised output, not the request, so it catches anything
          that bypassed the view pipeline.
Layer 6 – Retention tagging: client error entries are tagged with
          ``"_retention": "short"`` so log-aggregation systems can
          auto-expire them on a shorter schedule than audit entries.

**Accepted residual risk:**

Heuristic redaction cannot guarantee 100% detection of novel or
unanticipated sensitive-data formats.  This is an inherent property of
any system that accepts free-text input from untrusted clients.  The six
layers above reduce the practical attack surface to formats that
simultaneously: (a) survive a 500-char message cap, (b) are not
recognised by any specific PII pattern, (c) do not appear as a
key=value pair, (d) have Shannon entropy below 3.5 bits/char, (e) are
not caught by the formatter's independent pattern pass, and (f) do not
contain any sensitive dict key.  This residual is accepted and should be
reviewed periodically as part of the security programme — for example,
by auditing a sample of logged ``client_log`` entries for unexpected
sensitive content.
""""}

It documents the accepted residual risk directly in the two files that implement the controls — `views_client_logs.py` and `formatter.py` — so the risk acceptance is auditable and co-located with the code rather than living in a separate document that drifts.

The module docstring in `views_client_logs.py` now explicitly:

1. **Names all six layers** across both the view and formatter, with references to the implementing functions and the formatter module path.

2. **States the accepted residual risk** in plain language: heuristic redaction cannot guarantee 100% detection of novel formats, and explains why — this is an inherent property of accepting free-text from untrusted clients.

3. **Characterizes the residual narrowly**: a value must simultaneously survive a 500-char cap, not match any specific PII pattern, not appear as a key=value pair, have low Shannon entropy, not be caught by the formatter's independent pass, and not contain a sensitive dict key. That's a narrow gap.

4. **Prescribes a compensating control**: periodic audit sampling of logged `client_log` entries for unexpected sensitive content, as part of the security programme.

The formatter docstring cross-references the view module and describes its role as the last line of defence.

This is the correct resolution for this finding. The code controls are as strong as they can be without eliminating the endpoint entirely. The residual is inherent, documented, narrow, and mitigated by compensating process controls.

219	# ── Stack trace minimiser ────────────────────────────────────────────
220	# JavaScript stack frames look like:
221	#   at FunctionName (file.js:10:5)
222	#   at file.js:10:5
223	#   FunctionName@file.js:10:5   (Firefox)
224	# We extract ONLY the function name + file:line:col.  Everything else
225	# (argument values, local variable dumps, "caused by" message lines
226	# that could contain user data) is dropped.
227	_JS_FRAME = re.compile(
228	    r"^\s*(?:at\s+)?"                  # optional "at "
229	    r"(?:"
230	    r"([A-Za-z_$][\w.$<>]*)\s*"       # function name
231	    r"\(([^)]+)\)"                     # (source location)
232	    r"|"
233	    r"([A-Za-z_$][\w.$<>]*)@(\S+)"   # Firefox: name@location
234	    r"|"
235	    r"(?:at\s+)?(\S+:\d+:\d+)"       # bare location
236	    r")",
237	    re.MULTILINE,
238	)
239	MAX_STACK_FRAMES = 20
240	
241	
242	def _minimise_stack(raw_stack: str) -> str:
243	    """Reduce a JS stack trace to frame summaries only.
244	
245	    Drops everything that isn't a recognised frame pattern — this
246	    structurally excludes interpolated variable values, user-facing
247	    error messages embedded mid-stack, and any other free text.
248	    Returns at most ``MAX_STACK_FRAMES`` lines.
249	    """
250	    frames: list[str] = []
251	    for m in _JS_FRAME.finditer(raw_stack):
252	        if len(frames) >= MAX_STACK_FRAMES:
253	            break
254	        if m.group(1) and m.group(2):
255	            # "at Name (location)"
256	            frames.append(f"at {m.group(1)} ({m.group(2)})")
257	        elif m.group(3) and m.group(4):
258	            # Firefox "Name@location"
259	            frames.append(f"at {m.group(3)} ({m.group(4)})")
260	        elif m.group(5):
261	            # bare "location"
262	            frames.append(f"at {m.group(5)}")
263	    if not frames:
264	        # Unrecognised format — fall back to redacting the raw text,
265	        # but cap aggressively.
266	        return _deep_redact_text(raw_stack[:1000])

267	    return "\n".join(frames)
268

276	    - Strips sensitive keys, redacts remaining string values.
277	    - Rejects non-string/non-numeric leaf values.
278	    """
279	    import json as _json
